hook: You already know how to compare means and proportions for numerical data — but what happens when your data is made up of categories, like favorite music genres or survey responses? Standard tests won't cut it when you're asking 'does the distribution of responses match what we expected?' or 'are two categorical variables related to each other?' This unit gives you powerful tools to answer exactly those questions.
concept: The unifying idea is using observed versus expected counts to decide whether patterns in categorical data are due to a real effect or just random chance — formally captured by the $\chi^2$ (chi-square) family of tests.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to test whether a single categorical variable follows a claimed or theoretical distribution by comparing observed counts to expected counts using the $\chi^2$ statistic.
Apply the $\chi^2$ test to determine whether two categorical variables are associated with each other or are statistically independent within a population.
Master the construction and interpretation of two-way contingency tables and calculate expected cell counts using marginal totals to set up any chi-square analysis.
